key_concerns: Small safety database (<90 subjects), limiting detection of rare adverse events
Hyperglycemia risk, especially in diabetics, requiring glucose monitoring
Hearing impairment (hypoacusis, tinnitus) with unknown mechanism
Higher infection rate vs placebo without clear cause
Undefined 'Active' Thyroid Eye Disease criteria for indication
Unknown long-term efficacy and safety of repeat treatment courses
key_positives: Significant proptosis reduction: 82% vs 16% placebo (2 mm threshold)
No approved drug therapies for Thyroid Eye Disease
Clinically meaningful improvement in diplopia and corneal coverage
Favorable effect on non-study eye (68% reduction)
Inclusion of patient-reported outcomes and clinician-reported outcomes

evidence_notes: Efficacy claim: 'Eighty-two percent (82%) of patients... had at least a two-millimeter reduction in ptosis [likely proptosis] compared to only 16 percent' (Section: Efficacy)

Clinical significance: 'A two-millimeter reduction is considered clinically significant because it is expected to reduce the incidence of diplopia and improve the lid coverage over the cornea' (Section: Efficacy)
Limited database: 'Less than 90 subjects treated with infusions of teprotumumab have been enrolled in controlled clinical trials' (Section: Efficacy C)
Safety signal: 'Based on the rule of 3’s, with a safety database of 90 subjects, adverse events may be expected to occur at rates of 3% without the event being observed' (Section: Efficacy C)
Hyperglycemia: 'Three teprotumumab subjects with normal fasting glucose values at Baseline demonstrated elevated fasting glucose values for at least 1 visit' (Section: Efficacy E)
Infection rate: 'The reported infection rate associated with teprotumumab was 33%, and higher than that of the placebo control in both studies' (Section: Safety I)
Hearing impairment: 'At least five patients reported hypoacusis/loss of hearing... The mechanism of action for hypoacusis remains unknown' (Section: Safety G)

questions_framing.description: FDA briefing poses neutral benefit-risk question ('Do the potential benefits... outweigh the potential risks?') and requests labeling recommendations, indicating standard advisory committee framing focused on approval conditions rather than outright rejection.

Positives: Significant proptosis reduction: 82% vs 16% placebo (2 mm threshold); No approved drug therapies for Thyroid Eye Disease; Clinically meaningful improvement in diplopia and corneal coverage; Favorable effect on non-study eye (68% reduction); Inclusion of patient-reported outcomes and clinician-reported outcomes
